title: Are we one repo away from a community-built AI quant lab? Why isn’t anyone building an open-source AI quant collective?
author: Numerous_Pickle_9678
selftext:
I keep feeling like this should exist by now.

Not a public hedge fund on day one. Not “send money and trust the magic AI.” I mean an open-source AI quant collective that starts with research, backtesting, evals, and paper trading.

The basic idea:

* one shared repo
* contributors build strategies, signals, evals, risk systems, and agent workflows
* everything is transparent and open source
* nothing touches real capital until it survives hard gates
* contribution quality matters more than noise or popularity

I already have a public repo called `llm-quant`, and if people are genuinely interested I’m happy to turn that into the starting point and set up the infra needed. Right now it’s already a four-track systematic trading research program running over a $100k paper portfolio across 39 tradeable assets, with anti-overfitting gates and paper-trading promotion rules in place. ([GitHub](https://github.com/45ck/llm-quant))

What I’m wondering is whether a serious community version could work:

* shared research and experiment history
* paper-trading leaderboards
* contribution scoring
* agent-generated proposals
* human review for risky changes
* hard rules before anything gets promoted
* voting system for decisions based on capital invested and contribution to project (i.e. capital or inference) 

Would anyone here actually want to contribute to something like that if it started as an open paper-trading lab?

replies.body: My repo is cpu heavy it utilizes an LLM only for a filtering loop after every loop everything is validated with a cpu run so the more cores you have the better for this repo

comments.author: Equal-Ad5322
comments.body:
This is a great vision, and to answer your final question: Yes, people would absolutely contribute to this. But the reason we aren't just "one repo away" right now is an infrastructure problem. We are one *engine* away.

AI agents are excellent at generating strategy logic and evaluating signals, but if they are deploying that code onto a slow, poorly memory-managed backtester, the collective just becomes a graveyard of overfit prototypes. To actually enforce the "hard gates" and "human review for risky changes" you mentioned, you need extreme computational speed to validate their outputs.

I am currently building my own C# trading platform and research lab focused exactly on this kind of raw performance and memory efficiency. Right now, the engine processes raw tick data at \~3M ticks per second for a single backtest. During parallelized parameter sweeps, it hits 30M to 50M ticks per second. That speed is what allows you to sweep thousands of settings across years of tick data in minutes rather than days. Without that kind of throughput, the rigorous walk-forward testing required to protect a shared portfolio is practically impossible.

Over the next few months, my roadmap actually aligns perfectly with your "promotion rules" idea. I am building out:

1. A continuous Walk-Forward Optimization (WFO) orchestrator.
2. Hidden Markov Models for real-time market regime detection.
3. A "Shadow Trading Tournament" where strategies dynamically compete for capital allocation based on their performance in live micro-structure environments.

You mentioned keeping this open-source. While my core engine won't be strictly OSI open-source, I am preparing to release it under a Business Source License (BSL 1.1).

I think BSL is actually the most realistic license for the kind of collective you want to build. It gives other quants and developers full access to the source code to run their own research, build agent workflows, and contribute to the lab. However, it legally protects the architecture—meaning if a prop firm or hedge fund tries to take the community's infrastructure and turn it into a commercial business, the BSL ensures the original creators actually get their share.

comments.author: Admirable_Ad9599
comments.body: I am just a vibe coder but I think I kind of understand this. I would think the problem would be people just never agree on what is a “good idea”. I think for something like this to work you would still need one visionary type person to direct a project and set up bounties maybe and assign contribution points to each bounty which would determine equity.

comments.author: AlbatrossAwkward2994
comments.body: Can someone make something i can donate my idle 5090 and 4090s time to that trains public models?

comments.author: BackTesting-Queen
comments.body: Your idea sounds intriguing and I believe it has potential. I've been using a similar system for my own trading and it's been incredibly effective in refining my strategies and improving my overall trading performance.
